Gegebener geneset wird auf Anzahl und Namen der Pathwayübergruppen untersucht:

```{r}
new_genesets = genesets$genesets
names(new_genesets)
genesets$genesets$Angio_AACR
```

Computation of the Jaccards index: The Jaccard index is a statistical indicator used to measure the similarity between two sets. It is used here to quantify the similarity between different genesets.

The Jaccard index is defined as the size of the intersection of the two sets divided by the size of the union of the two sets:

$$ J(A, B) = \frac{|A \cap B|}{|A \cup B|} $$

![](C:/Users/kolbc/Downloads/jaccard.jpg)

Here $A$ stands for the first set, $B$ for the second set, $|A \cap B|$ for the number of elements contained in both sets (intersection set), and $|A \cup B|$ for the number of elements contained in at least one of the two sets (union set).

The Jaccard index provides a value between 0 and 1, where 0 means that the two sets have no common elements and 1 means that the two sets are identical. A higher Jaccard index indicates a greater similarity between the sets.

```{r}
jaccard <- function(a, b) {
    intersection = length(intersect(a, b))
    union = length(union(a,b))
    return (intersection/union)
}
```

create similarity matrix:

```{r}
similarity_matrix = matrix(0, nrow = length(new_genesets), ncol = length(new_genesets))
rownames(similarity_matrix) = colnames(similarity_matrix) = names(new_genesets)
for (i in 1:length(new_genesets)) {
  for (j in 1:length(new_genesets)) {
    similarity_matrix[i, j] = jaccard(new_genesets[[i]], new_genesets[[j]])
  }
}
```

Plotting the result of the jaccard similarities in a heatmap:

```{r}
names = c(new_genesets)
library(pheatmap)
pheatmap(as.matrix(similarity_matrix),
      cellwidth = 5, cellheight = 5,
        cluster_rows = F,
         cluster_cols = F,
         fontsize = 5,
          main = "Jaccard Similarity Heatmap")
```
low similarity amongs the different data sets can be observed

Extraction of those gene sets with elevated similarity:
```{r}
# Define a threshold for elevated similarity (in this case 50% overlap)
threshold <- 0.5
# Find the indices of elements with similarity above the threshold and below 1
high_similarity_indices <- which(similarity_matrix > threshold & similarity_matrix < 1, arr.ind = TRUE)
# Extract the indices of rows and columns with elevated similarity
row_indices <- high_similarity_indices[, 1]
col_indices <- high_similarity_indices[, 2]

row_gene_set_names <- names(new_genesets)[row_indices]
col_gene_set_names <- names(new_genesets)[col_indices]
# Extract the corresponding elements with elevated similarity
elevated_similarity_gene_sets <- data.frame(
  RowGeneSet = row_gene_set_names,
  ColumnGeneSet = col_gene_set_names,
  SimilarityValue = similarity_matrix[high_similarity_indices]
)
# Print or use the extracted information as needed
print(elevated_similarity_gene_sets)

```
This shows 16 pairs with elevated similarity above threshold. However there are only 8 unique pairs carrying at least partially redundand information.
